For disaster recovery, this directory should be located on a different physical drive than where the data resides.  Each snapshot will consume approximately the same amount of space as the data directory. The backup script does not perform any directory maintenance or purging. This can be done either by using a wrapper script which will delete the current backup before creating the new snapshot, or by a scheduled task which is to be executed before the backup. If point in time recovery is desired, the backup directory can be backed up as part of the regular backup procedures for the server.</p><p>When backing up a cluster, all nodes must be backed up individually, preferably at the same time. <strong>Also, on a cluster, it is imperative that the clocks on all machines be synchronized.</strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5b410d9f-9414-467d-b3b2-fd62a0e88135"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Hot backups should be executed at a time when there is no client activity, in order to ensure model consistency. If you cannot be sure that there is no client traffic at the time of the snapshot, please stop the Teamwork Cloud service momentarily. Once the “nodetool snapshot” command has completed, you can restart the Teamwork Cloud service.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Restore</h4><p>The restore procedure will stop the Cassandra database, delete the commit logs and the data files in the repository, restore the snapshot files and relocate them back to their source directories. Once this is done, Cassandra will be started and the nodetool utility will be invoked to repair the keyspaces. </p><p>When restoring a cluster, all nodes must be restored from their individual backups (backups taken at the same time). You must first stop the Teamwork Cloud, authserver, and Cassandra service on each of the nodes. Once the Cassandra nodes are off, proceed to restore the seed node. Once you have completed the restoration of the seed node, proceed to restore the second node. Upon completion of the second node, restore the third node. During the restoration process of the first 2 nodes, you may receive an error from the keyspace repair process - this is normal since some of the nodes have not joined the cluster. The last node to be restored will take care of the keyspace repairing process.</p><h3>Backup and restore scripts</h3><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="37dc3ba8-c974-4c91-9997-85ad55cbd582"><ac:parameter ac:name="">scripts</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p>To backup and restore the Cassandra database, you need the <em>backup.sh </em>and <em>restore-single_node.sh </em>files from the <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">backup_restore_scripts_&lt;version&gt;.zip</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">script package. </span>Download<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> the package <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/Downloadable+scripts">here</a></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><h4>backup.sh</h4><p>This script creates snapshots of Cassandra database. The script must run on the host running Cassandra and Cassandra must be running. The Cassandra bin directory or nodetool must be added to <strong>$PATH</strong> (on Linux, nodetool is available when the Cassandra package is installed without further modifications). The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner. On Linux, you should be running on a shell with root permissions.</p><p>The command format for backing up is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f8ba3a96-d1f9-4f14-bffe-79fb1fe998c7"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[./backup.sh -dir CASSANDRA_DB_PATH -rf BACKUP_DIR]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>where <strong>CASSANDRA_DB_PATH</strong> is the directory below where the Cassandra data is located, and <strong>BACKUP_DIR</strong> is the location in which the snapshot archives are to be saved.</p><p>The archive is named using a pattern <strong>cassandra_backup_yyyy.mm.dd-hh.mm.ss.tar</strong></p><p>If any of the parameters are omitted then the script will prompt you for the locations, and perform sanity checks to make sure the values are correct.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0bdf87f2-5085-4c99-b153-be5833f7b621"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Path names</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When calling the backup/restore scripts passing parameters, if your path names contain spaces, they must be quoted.</p><p>For example:</p><p>./backup.sh -dir &quot;/path to data containing spaces/data&quot; -rf &quot;/path to backups containing spaces/&quot;</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>restore-single_node.sh</h4><p>This script should be run as root on Linux and Cygwin console must be launched with the administrator privileges. This script restores data from the backup file. While restoring Cassandra, Teamwork Cloud server must be turned off. The script stops and starts Cassandra if needed. The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner, and then restores backup on another machine with another user. Therefore, the file permissions must be changed manually otherwise Cassandra may not start. The following variables may be passed onto the script:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bf8b5e6c-4f61-4ac5-aeab-f58d1034e516"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Usage: ./restore-single_node.sh [--dir <arg>] [--commitlog <arg>] [--rf <arg>] [--cassandra <arg>] [-h|--help]
--dir: cassandra database path (default:cassandra_database)
--commitlog: commitlog path (default: commitlog_path)
--rf: full path to snapshot archive (default: rf)
--cassandra: cassandra installation path when cassandra is not executed as a service (default: cassandra)
-h,--help: Prints help]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Even though parameters can be passed to the restore script, restores are typically carried out in an interactive manner.</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup class=""><col class="" /><col class="" /></colgroup><tbody class=""><tr class=""><th>Variable</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>commitlog_path</strong></td><td>The path of commitlog files. They are removed when restoring database to prevent recovery of commits made earlier. DataStax recommends to store the commitlog files in a separate hard-disk for performance sake.</td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>cassandra_database</strong></td><td>The Cassandra database path. It is used to clean existing database and restoring database from the recovery file.</td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>cassandra</strong></td><td>The Cassandra installation path. It is the required parameter when Cassandra is launched not as service.</td></tr><tr class=""><td colspan="1"><strong>service</strong></td><td colspan="1">The Boolean parameter indicating that Cassandra is started as a service. The default value is true so you need to set this variable to <em>false</em> when Cassandra is launched by the other script or manually.</td></tr><tr class=""><td colspan="1"><strong>rf</strong></td><td colspan="1">The recovery file with the full path to it.</td></tr></tbody></table><h4><strong>On Linux</strong></h4><p>Type: </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9227c5ec-45ce-40da-9d33-37928be9e017"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ su ./restore-single_node.sh ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">or</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06f669d0-dce6-4748-9ac2-7902a51e4326"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ sudo su
$ ./restore-single_node.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you are logged in as root, the command is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9988a4a2-2a97-4cb7-8457-5ae3ef3970ef"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ ./restore-single_node.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247047601 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00605: Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud

- page_id: `247047601`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247047601/Cameo+Collaborator+for+Teamwork+Cloud
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-11T16:48:41.395+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud is a web-based product designed to present models in a simplified form for stakeholders, sponsors, customers, and engineering teams. It allows editing and reviewing models. The product is built with scalability in mind, making it possible to deploy it both in a single node and a cluster setting. It works in all form factors (desktop, tablet, and smart phones), making model sharing and reviewing easy.

Watch the following video to see Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud in action:

800450

Use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud to:

- Present models to stakeholders, sponsors, or customers.
- Present models to engineering teams (modelers and non-modelers) within your organization.
- Keep stakeholders and engineering teams up-to-date with the precise, consistent information from the single source of truth - model repository.
- Review models and write comments to provide feedback to model authors.
- Address comments by making changes to models without using a modeling tool.
- Edit models on the web without having to use a modeling tool, e.g., for specification of interface libraries or editing requirements

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The conceptual schema of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.

To learn how to install an use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, refer to the following topics:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It allows editing and reviewing models. The product is built with scalability in mind, making it possible to deploy it both in a single node and a cluster setting. It works in all form factors (desktop, tablet, and smart phones), making model sharing and reviewing easy.</p><p style="text-align: left;">Watch the following video to see Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud in action:<br /><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="41338db9-c745-4d83-a11f-a0eb646f7bcd"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">800</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YE6GCoJVHeg" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">450</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p class="bodytext" style="text-align: left;">Use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud to:</p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><p>Present models to stakeholders, sponsors, or customers.</p></li><li><p>Present models to engineering teams (modelers and non-modelers) within your organization.</p></li><li><p>Keep stakeholders and engineering teams up-to-date with the precise, consistent information from the single source of truth - model repository.</p></li><li><p>Review models and write comments to provide feedback to model authors.</p></li><li><p>Address comments by making changes to models without using a modeling tool.</p></li><li><p>Edit models on the web without having to use a modeling tool, e.g., for specification of interface libraries or editing requirements</p></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="high_level_architecture.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The conceptual schema of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.</h6><p>To learn how to install an use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, refer to the following topics:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="1af390c3-7a92-4d14-bb6a-49fc02d6d986" /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046620 space=MCS version=13 -->
## PAGE 00607: Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud licensing

- page_id: `247046620`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046620/Cameo+Collaborator+for+Teamwork+Cloud+licensing
- version_number: 13
- version_date: `2026-03-25T14:43:57.818+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > License management
- labels: ['twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### License types

You can purchase the following types of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud licenses, depending on the number of connections you want to use:

| License types | Authenticated commenting | Anonymous commenting | Number of concurrent authenticated connections | Access to content shared for anonymous use* | Predefined portals | Customized portals |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 5-connection license | + | - | 5 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 10-connection license | + | - | 10 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 25-connection license | + | - | 25 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 50-connection license | + | - | 50 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 75-connection license | + | - | 75 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 100-connection license | + | - | 100 | Unlimited | + | + |
| Unlimited license | + | + | Unlimited | Unlimited | + | + |

***** Unauthenticated users can open Cameo Collaborator documents via [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sharing document links' space=''].

##### Applying licenses to new installations (no existing license server)

Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses a floating license managed by the FlexNet license server or DSLS (Dassault Systèmes License Server). The license server information is specified when installing Web Application Platform with the Cameo Collaborator service, so make sure that you install a license server before installing Web Application Platform. The steps below explain how to apply the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license and start using the product.

To apply the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license

1. Install and start one of the following license servers:
  - [DSLS](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=dsls-installation-and-licensing&version=latest)
  - [FlexNet](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-license-server-installation-and-licensing&version=latest)
2. Check if the license server is running.
3. Add the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license to the license server.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Web Application Platform Installation on a separate machine' space=''] with the Cameo Collaborator service and specify the license server details during the installation.

Once you apply the license as described above, the product is ready to use. Note that you can change the license server details later if needed.

#### WARNING: Working with multiple licenses

Working with multiple licenses

The FlexNet license server can contain only one license. If you want to add multiple licenses to the same FlexNet license server (e.g., Cameo Collaborator, Teamwork Cloud, and MagicDraw), contact your sales executive and ask them to merge the licenses.

##### Managing licenses via Teamwork Cloud Admin

There are multiple ways to manage Cameo Collaborator licenses

#### NOTE: Multiple pods' licensing

Multiple pods' licensing

If you have multiple pods (i.e. Kubernetes), you can configure all licenses either via the*webappplatform**.properties*file OR via the user interface in the **Settings**application.

If you want to manage licenses via the user interface in the **Settings**application, but at least one pod has the license configured via the *webapp.properties*file, you need to remove the license from the *webapp.properties*file and restart the services.

###### Applying a license via Teamwork Cloud Admin

To apply a Cameo Collaborator license via Teamwork Cloud Admin

1. Open Teamwork Cloud in a web browser using this address format: http(s)://<server_address>:<port>/webapp . 8443 is the default port.
2. Sign in to Teamwork Cloud with a user who has the Server Administrator role .
3. Click the Settings application to open it.
4. From the left-side menu, select the Cameo Collaborator license page. [ATTACHMENT filename='cc_license_in_webapp.png']
5. Select either the FlexNet or DSLS tab:
  - In the FlexNet tab, enter the license server address to view the list of available licenses. [ATTACHMENT filename='cc_flexnet_license.png'] Select a license and click Confirm to apply.
  - In the DSLS tab, select either Organization defined or Custom.
    - If you select Organization defined, choose a license from the list and click Confirm to apply.
    - If you select Custom, enter the license server address, choose a license from the list and click Confirm to apply.

###### Viewing active users

The Cameo Collaborator license page allows you to view active connections (users). They are displayed in the **Recently active connections**part of the page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Managing licenses via the properties file

###### Checking which license is in use

You can check Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license information either or as described below.

To check the license information

1. In your internet browser, go to *http(s)://<server_address>:<port>/webapp*. Default portBy default, Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses port 8443.
2. If you are not signed in, enter your user credentials on the authentication page.
3. In the same internet browser, go to *http(s)://<domain_name>:<port>/collaborator/api/collaborator/license/info*

The web page displays Cameo Collaborator license details, including the number of connections and expiration date.

###### Specifying which license should be used

If there are several licenses in the license server, you can specify which Cameo Collaborator license (edition) should be used.

To specify which Cameo Collaborator license should be used

1. Go to the *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf* directory.
2. Open for editing the *webappplatform.properties* file.
3. In the file, add the **cc.license.edition** property and specify its value (e.g., **cc.license.edition=100+ Connections** for an unlimited license). Valid property valuesThe valid values for the **cc.license.edition** property are:5 Connections10 Connections25 Connections50 Connections75 Connections100 Connections100+ Connections
4. Restart the Web Application Platform.

###### Resetting a license

The instructions below explain how to reset the Cameo Collaborator license if, for example, the connection with the license server was lost.

To reset the Cameo Collaborator license

1. In an internet browser, go to *http(s)://<server_address>:<port>/webapp*. Default portBy default, Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses port 8443.
2. If you are not signed in, enter your user credentials on the authentication page.
3. In the same internet browser, go to *http(s)://<domain_name>:<port>/collaborator/api/collaborator/license/reset*

**If the license reset was successful, the open web page will display this message:

{"initialized":true}

###### Specifying license server information for Web Application Platform

The licensing information should be specified when installing Teamwork Cloud and/or Web Application Platform. If you have not specified licensing information during installation or if you want to change it later, you can do this by following the steps below.

To specify/change the license server information

1. Go to the *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf* directory.
2. Open for editing the *webappplatform.properties* file. In the *webappplatform.properties* file:The values of some properties are placeholders, e.g., **mail.from.default=info@company.com**. Make sure to replace the placeholders with actual values.Read the comments with property descriptions to ensure accuracy when specifying property values.
3. Specify the following properties under the FlexNet/DSLS server properties for Web Application Platform services property group.
  - For the DSLS server:
    - **license.framework**
    - **license.server**
    - **license.dsls.server.mode**
  - For the FlexNet server:
    - **license.framework**
    - **license.server** Cameo Collaborator supports the [FlexNet Three-Server Redundancy mode (Triad License Server)](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-three-server-redundancy---triad-license-server&version=latest). If you want to use the Triad License Server, specify the **license.server** property in this format:license.server=<port>@<server1_address>,<port>@<server2_address>,<port>@<server3_address>
4. Restart Web Application Platform.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 84.0922%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 19.6478%;" /><col style="width: 11.5537%;" /><col style="width: 9.53017%;" /><col style="width: 15.1438%;" /><col style="width: 17.8854%;" /><col style="width: 13.512%;" /><col style="width: 12.7271%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="text-align: center;"><p>License types</p></th><th style="text-align: connections</p></th><th style="text-align: center;"><p>Access to content shared for anonymous use*</p></th><th style="text-align: center;"><p>Predefined portals</p></th><th style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>75-connection license</strong></p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>-</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>100-connection license</strong></p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>-</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>100</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Unlimited</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Unlimited license</strong></p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Unlimited</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Unlimited</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td><td style="text-align: center;"><p>+</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>*</strong> Unauthenticated users can open Cameo Collaborator documents via <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sharing document links" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[public links]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h3>Applying licenses to new installations (no existing license server)</h3><p class="auto-cursor-target">Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses a floating license managed by the FlexNet license server or <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">DSLS (Dassault Systèmes License Server)</span>. The license server information is specified when installing Web Application Platform with the Cameo Collaborator service, so make sure that you install a license server before installing Web Application Platform. The steps below explain how to apply the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license and start using the product.</p><p><br /></p><p>To apply the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="fe825fb1-2c54-4494-a709-3622b65a2ce6"><p>Install and start one of the following license servers:</p><ul><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=dsls-installation-and-licensing&amp;version=latest">DSLS</a></span></p></li><li><p><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-license-server-installation-and-licensing&amp;version=latest">FlexNet</a></p></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="a2915bbf-2e91-4fd4-9bfe-4c00f6bbe08e"><p>Check if the license server is running.</p></li><li data-uuid="aefaa230-5bd0-4f65-a73b-6074740150ba"><p>Add the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license to the license server.</p></li><li data-uuid="2e4011b2-f68c-4c9a-8436-ef9f9b6ef77f"><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Web Application Platform Installation on a separate machine" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install Web Aplication Platform]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> with the Cameo Collaborator service and specify the license server details during the installation.</p></li></ol><p>Once you apply the license as described above, the product is ready to use. Note that you can change the license server details later if needed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4e978a07-965f-415c-a1af-b7eb46839f02"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Working with multiple licenses</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The FlexNet license server can contain only one license. If you want to add multiple licenses <span>to the same FlexNet license server</span> (e.g., Cameo Collaborator, Teamwork Cloud, and MagicDraw), contact your sales executive and ask them to merge the licenses.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Managing  licenses via Teamwork Cloud Admin</h3><p>There are multiple ways to manage Cameo Collaborator licenses</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfa1d0d2-333e-4cd0-b197-f14e5469b0df"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Multiple pods' licensing</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: left;">If you have multiple pods (i.e. Kubernetes), you can configure all licenses either via the<em style="text-align: left;">webappplatform</em><em>.properties </em>file OR via the user interface in the <strong>Settings </strong>application.</p><p style="text-align: left;">If you want to manage licenses via the user interface in the <strong>Settings </strong>application, but at least one pod has the license configured via the <em>webapp.properties </em>file, you need to remove the license from the <em>webapp.properties </em>file and restart the services.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Applying a license via Teamwork Cloud Admin</h4><p>To apply a Cameo Collaborator license via Teamwork Cloud Admin</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="482fdcad-e238-4144-a9a4-3a4d74f00445">Open Teamwork Cloud in a web browser using this address format: <em style="text-align: left;">http(s)://&lt;server_address&gt;:&lt;port&gt;/webapp</em>. 8443 is the default port.</li><li data-uuid="ef90b35e-4a03-4fc0-b456-f815b72fe97e"><span data-ref="97f3e5de-0d4f-4ad8-8602-ee5325800dad">Sign in to Teamwork Cloud with a user who has the Server Administrator role</span><span data-ref="97f3e5de-0d4f-4ad8-8602-ee5325800dad">.</span> </li><li data-uuid="0d5e49ab-5dfa-41ae-b9c2-8381a7eba5cb">Click the Settings application to open it.</li><li data-uuid="eac94b3b-f076-4573-baaa-8d5ea78d9a7e">From the left-side menu, select the Cameo Collaborator license page.<br /><ac:image ac:border="true"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cc_license_in_webapp.png" /></ac:image></li><li data-uuid="9e2ebadb-8fe5-49b5-aec5-e28d25fe7233">Select either the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-license-server-installation-and-licensing&amp;version=latest">FlexNet </a>or <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=dsls-installation-and-licensing&amp;version=latest">DSLS</a> tab:<ul><li>In the FlexNet tab, enter the license server address to view the list of available licenses.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="cc_flexnet_license.png" /></ac:image><br />Select a license and click <strong>Confirm </strong>to apply.</li><li>In the <strong>DSLS</strong> tab, select either <strong>Organization defined</strong> or <strong>Custom.</strong><ul><li>If you select <strong>Organization defined,</strong> choose a license from the list and click <strong>Confirm </strong>to apply.</li><li>If you select<strong> Custom, </strong>enter the license server address, choose a license from the list and click <strong>Confirm </strong><span>to apply.</span></li></ul></li></ul></li></ol><h4>Viewing active users</h4><p><span>The Cameo Collaborator license page allows you to view active connections (users). They are displayed in the <strong>Recently active connections </strong>part of the page. </span></p><p><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="cc_license_active_connections.png" /></ac:image></span></p><p><br /></p><h3>Managing licenses via the properties file</h3><h4><span>Checking which license is in use</span></h4><p>You can check Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license information either <ac:link ac:anchor="Applying a license through Teamwork Cloud Admin"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[in the Settings app]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To check the license information</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="18d462e5-35ec-496d-987e-b63b332ae978"><p class="auto-cursor-target"> In your internet browser, go to <em style="text-align: left;">http(s)://&lt;server_address&gt;:&lt;port&gt;/webapp</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fe1482e9-c4ab-4a93-ae27-618dcb8a6d1d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Default port</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>By default, Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses port 8443.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="ea2981ee-b4b3-4ce5-9bce-60d391c944f3"><p>If you are not signed in, enter your user credentials on the authentication page.</p></li><li data-uuid="db555e05-838e-4673-af5b-de3bcd59fd63"><p>In the same internet browser, go to <em>http(s)://&lt;domain_name&gt;:&lt;port&gt;/collaborator/api/collaborator/license/info</em></p></li></ol><p>The web page displays Cameo Collaborator license details, including the number of connections and expiration date.</p><h4>Specifying which license should be used</h4><p>If there are several licenses in the license server, you can specify which Cameo Collaborator license (edition) should be used.</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify which Cameo Collaborator license should be used</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="1ed8af92-ab53-4dd6-8d9b-d1a713b301ff"><p>Go to the <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf</em> directory.</p></li><li data-uuid="8912b841-36ec-45e3-91ac-46ed6c1ebaad"><p>Open for editing the <em>webappplatform.properties</em> file.</p></li><li data-uuid="799f03b0-221d-4c17-9af9-ecd815eb1bad"><p>In the file, add the <strong>cc.license.edition</strong> property and specify its value (e.g., <strong>cc.license.edition=100+ Connections</strong> for an unlimited license).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08653ab3-0b85-4ce8-9b5f-14a6cc65c9f6"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Valid property values</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The valid values for the <strong>cc.license.edition</strong> property are:</p><ul><li><p>5 Connections</p></li><li>10 Connections</li><li><p>25 Connections</p></li><li><p>50 Connections</p></li><li><p>75 Connections</p></li><li><p>100 Connections</p></li><li><p>100+ Connections</p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="c0234653-73a1-4af5-9b0c-63a632899550"><p>Restart the Web Application Platform.</p></li></ol><h4>Resetting a license</h4><p>The instructions below explain how to reset the Cameo Collaborator license if, for example, the connection with the license server was lost.</p><p><br /></p><p>To reset the Cameo Collaborator license</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="e3a1d477-68d2-459e-9f7e-960a7b712c3d"><p class="auto-cursor-target"> In an internet browser, go to <em style="text-align: left;">http(s)://&lt;server_address&gt;:&lt;port&gt;/webapp</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb51825a-badf-4eed-b217-db90141c5a6d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Default port</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>By default, Cameo Collabora</p><p>tor for Teamwork Cloud uses port 8443.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="c8c971ec-3aef-4208-977b-87aeb2125ef0"><p>If you are not signed in, enter your user credentials on the authentication page.</p></li><li data-uuid="212e017f-dc6c-46b3-a729-467f7b383fdd"><p>In the same internet browser, go to <em>http(s)://&lt;domain_name&gt;:&lt;port&gt;/collaborator/api/collaborator/license/reset</em></p></li></ol><p><em> </em>If the license reset was successful, the open web page will display this message:</p><p>{&quot;initialized&quot;:true}</p><h4>Specifying license server information for Web Application Platform</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The licensing information should be specified when installing Teamwork Cloud and/or Web Application Platform. If you have not specified licensing information during installation or if you want to change it later, you can do this by following the steps below.</span></p><p><br /></p><p>To specify/change the l<span>icense server information</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="c93596cd-4dc1-4841-a245-d84500b3a244"><p>Go to the <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf</em> directory.</p></li><li data-uuid="fa66d05e-c3cc-45b1-883b-9d3f7b21066c"><p>Open for editing the <em>webappplatform.properties</em> file.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e24de815-3242-4ef4-8043-eedd63e07e43"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <em>webappplatform.properties</em> file:</span></p><ul><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The values of some properties are placeholders, e.g., <strong>mail.from.default=info@company.com</strong>. Make sure to replace the placeholders with actual values.</span></p></li><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Read the comments with property descriptions to ensure accuracy when specifying property values.</span></p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="41c7f86b-12b5-4c6b-bf79-3677d951afbc">Specify the following properties under the <em>FlexNet/DSLS server properties for Web Application Platform services</em> property group.<ul><li><p>For the DSLS server:</p><ul><li><p><strong>license.framework</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>license.server</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>license.dsls.server.mode</strong></p></li></ul></li><li><p>For the FlexNet server:</p><ul><li><p><strong>license.framework</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>license.server<br /></strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7db8618e-5799-4302-9e90-687cd73d1271"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Cameo Collaborator supports the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-three-server-redundancy---triad-license-server&amp;version=latest">FlexNet Three-Server Redundancy mode (Triad License Server)</a>. If you want to use the Triad License Server, specify the <strong>license.server</strong> property in this format:</p><p>license.server=&lt;port&gt;@&lt;server1_address&gt;,&lt;port&gt;@&lt;server2_address&gt;,&lt;port&gt;@&lt;server3_address&gt;</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="b457b3e9-3803-42c9-a6ce-601a43838eca"><p>Restart Web Application Platform.</p></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247047252 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00608: Case study

- page_id: `247047252`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247047252/Case+study
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-11T16:48:35.368+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Admin web applications > Roles application > Role-Based Access Control concept
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This case study presents a simple situation in an organization where users are classified into two groups: Team A and Team B according to their type. Team A is a group of users imported from an LDAP server and Team B is a group of internal users created using Teamwork Cloud Admin. This case study is used to illustrate how roles are assigned to each user in each team to work on different projects.

The following figure illustrates the two groups of users in the organization, Team A and Team B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The users are grouped into Team A (external users) and Team B (internal users).

There are three projects for the teams to work on: Project A, Project B, and Project C.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Project A is assigned to Team A, Project B is assigned to Team B, and Project C is assigned to both Team A and Team B.

Before starting to work on the projects, the organization needs to create a proper role and scope for everyone in the teams so that it can divide the tasks among them. The following figures show the team members' roles in Project A and Project B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A specific role is assigned to each member in Team A in Project A and Team B in Project B.

Since Project C is the largest, the organization decided to assign it to both Team A and Team B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Besides working on their respective project, both teams are also responsible for Project C.

More roles are created to manage the team members and the system. The following figure, for example, shows the user u7's roles as both User Manager and Security Manager, and the u8's role as a Project Creator. The user u7's role is to manage all users and access so the right scope to assign is **Global**. The user u8's role is to create projects so the right scope to assign is also **Global**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The role scope of the user u7 and user u8 is Global.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This case study presents a simple situation in an organization where users are classified into two groups: Team A and Team B according to their type. Team A is a group of users imported from an LDAP server and Team B is a group of internal users created using Teamwork Cloud Admin. This case study is used to illustrate how roles are assigned to each user in each team to work on different projects.</p><p>The following figure illustrates the two groups of users in the organization, Team A and Team B.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Case study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The users are grouped into Team A (external users) and Team B (internal users).</h6><p>There are three projects for the teams to work on: Project A, Project B, and Project C. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Case study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Project A is assigned to Team A, Project B is assigned to Team B, and Project C is assigned to both Team A and Team B.</h6><p>Before starting to work on the projects, the organization needs to create a proper role and scope for everyone in the teams so that it can divide the tasks among them. The following figures show the team members' roles in Project A and Project B.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy3.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Case study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A specific role is assigned to each member in Team A in Project A and Team B in Project B.</h6><p>Since Project C is the largest, the organization decided to assign it to both Team A and Team B.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy4.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Case study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Besides working on their respective project, both teams are also responsible for Project C.</h6><p>More roles are created to manage the team members and the system. The following figure, for example, shows the user u7's roles as both User Manager and Security Manager, and the u8's role as a Project Creator. The user u7's role is to manage all users and access so the right scope to assign is <strong>Global</strong>. The user u8's role is to create projects so the right scope to assign is also <strong>Global</strong>.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy5.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Case study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The role scope of the user u7 and user u8 is Global.</h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046526 space=MCS version=5 -->
## PAGE 00609: Cassandra and the product cluster setup

- page_id: `247046526`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046526/Cassandra+and+the+product+cluster+setup
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-12-03T14:26:37.139+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Installation > Advanced installation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page provides instructions to set up a Teamwork Cloud cluster on your system. A Teamwork Cloud cluster is composed of two clustering layers – Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra.

To set up a Teamwork Cloud cluster, you need to perform two separate tasks:

1. 
2. 

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An illustration of a Teamwork Cloud cluster and Cassandra cluster nodes within the Teamwork Cloud cluster.

##### Setting up a Cassandra cluster

Prior to establishing a Cassandra cluster, you need to determine the following.

- The initial number of nodes in the cluster.
- The IP address of each node.
- Determine which node will be the contact point.

The minimum recommended cluster size for Cassandra is 3 nodes, when taking availability and redundancy into account. Cassandra's default read and write consistency is quorum. Replication factor should match the minimum node count of 3. It is recommended to scale up a cluster in multiples of 3.

When setting up a Cassandra node, you need to configure a seed that indicates an initial contact point for the new node. So, you can configure the contact point using the IP of any existing active node in the cluster. Use the node's own IP when configuring the first node. For example, if you are configuring a three-node cluster and the IPs of the nodes are*10.1.1.101*,*10.1.1.102,*and*10.1.1.103,*select one of them (e.g.*10.1.1.101*) as the**seed**. When configuring**cassandra.yaml**, specify*10.1.1.101*as the contact point value for all of the three nodes.

| Machine IP | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| seeds | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 |
| listen_address | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |
| broadcast_rpc_address | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |

Note that

listen_address

and

broadcast_rpc_address

are still set to their respective machine’s address

Now that you understand what a seed is and how to configure it, follow the instructions to install and configure Cassandra on Windows or Linux operating system.

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Cassandra on Windows' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Apache Cassandra' space='']

After installing and configuring all nodes, start the seed node. Verify that it is operational by issuing the command "nodetool status".

Once the seed node is operational, you can proceed to start the other nodes in the cluster. You must allow the node to join the cluster before proceeding to the next node. After all nodes have started, the "nodetool status" command will display results as shown below.

```text

```

#### NOTE: Node Consistency

Node Consistency

In order to ensure that all nodes are consistent, please run the "nodetool repair" command nightly. This can be scheduled as a crontab job or via Windows Task Scheduler. This only needs to be executed on a single node.

###### Cassandra configuration parameters****

*-***esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver.basic.contact-points**

The value of this parameter is a list of Cassandra node IP addresses. You can find this parameter in **application.conf**by looking for the following.

```text

```

*-***esi.persistence.cassandra.keyspace.replication-factor**

This parameter defines the Cassandra replication factor which describes how many copies of your data will be written by Cassandra. For example, replication factor 2 means your data will be written to 2 nodes.

For a three-node cluster, if you would like the cluster to be able to survive 1 node loss, you will need to set the replication factor to 3.

```text

```

Please note that this configuration will be used only for the first time Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudconnects to Cassandra and creates a new twc keyspace. Changing the replication factor after the keyspace has already been created is rather a complex task. Read [this document](https://docs.datastax.com/en/cql/3.3/cql/cql_using/useUpdateKeyspaceRF.html) if you need to change it.

Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Clouduses QUORUM for both write and read consistency levels.

[Click here](https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra/3.0/cassandra/dml/dmlAboutDataConsistency.html) for a detailed explanation of data consistency.

To start up the Teamwork Cloud cluster, start the server on the seed machine and wait until you see a message similar to the following in the **server.log**.

```text

```

Then you can start the server on the remaining machines. You should see the following messages in the **server.log** which shows all 3 nodes are forming the cluster.

```text

```

##### Setting up a product cluster

Before setting up a Teamwork Cloud cluster, you need to determine the following.

- The initial number of nodes in the cluster.
- The IP address of each Teamwork Cloud node.
- Prepare a list of IPs of Cassandra nodes.

The instructions below use the following sample environment for ease of understanding.

- 3 nodes Teamwork Cloud cluster.
- Teamwork Cloud node IP addresses are 10.1.1.111, 10.1.1.112 , and 10.1.1.113.
- All nodes will be used as seed nodes.
- IP addresses of Cassandra nodes are 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102, 10.1.1.103.

Setting up the Teamwork Cloud cluster involves 2 groups of parameters in **application.conf**.

###### Teamwork Cloud clustering parameter

*-***akka.cluster.seed-nodes**

This parameter indicates the initial seed for the cluster.

If you install Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudusing the installer file, you will be asked to provide the seed node IP during installation process and the value will be configured in **application.conf**.

If you install using the zip file, you will need to manually configure the parameter in****application.conf**. Search for the following:

```text

```

Replace *${seed-node.ip}*with the IP addresses of the seed nodes, so it should look similar to the following:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This page provides instructions to set up a Teamwork Cloud cluster on your system. A Teamwork Cloud cluster is composed of two clustering layers – Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra.</p><p>To set up a Teamwork Cloud cluster, you need to perform two separate tasks:</p><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Setting up a Cassandra cluster"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Set up a Cassandra cluster]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Setting up a Teamwork Cloud cluster"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Set up a Teamwork Cloud cluster]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="teamwork_cloud_cluster.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An illustration of a Teamwork Cloud cluster and Cassandra cluster nodes within the Teamwork Cloud cluster.</h6><h3>Setting up a Cassandra cluster</h3><p>Prior to establishing a Cassandra cluster, you need to determine the following.</p><ul><li>The initial number of nodes in the cluster.</li><li>The IP address of each node.</li><li>Determine which node will be the contact point.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b27a4cad-02d2-472d-ace0-bc5ce80dd311"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The minimum recommended cluster size for Cassandra is 3 nodes, when taking availability and redundancy into account. Cassandra's default read and write consistency is quorum. Replication factor should match the minimum node count of 3. It is recommended to scale up a cluster in multiples of 3.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">When setting up a Cassandra node, you need to configure a seed that indicates an initial contact point for the new node. So, you can configure the contact point using the IP of any existing active node in the cluster. Use the node's own IP when configuring the first node. For example, if you are configuring a three-node cluster and the IPs of the nodes are </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">10.1.1.101</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">10.1.1.102,</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> and </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">10.1.1.103,</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> select one of them (e.g. </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">10.1.1.101</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">) as the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">seed</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. When configuring </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">cassandra.yaml</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, specify </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">10.1.1.101</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> as the contact point value for all of the three nodes.</span></p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Machine IP</th><th>10.1.1.101</th><th>10.1.1.102</th><th>10.1.1.103</th></tr><tr><td>seeds</td><td><strong>10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102</strong></td><td><strong>10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102</strong></td><td><strong>10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102</strong></td></tr><tr><td>listen_address</td><td><span>10.1.1.101</span></td><td><span>10.1.1.102</span></td><td><span>10.1.1.103</span></td></tr><tr><td>broadcast_rpc_address</td><td><span>10.1.1.101</span></td><td><span>10.1.1.102</span></td><td><span>10.1.1.103</span></td></tr></tbody></table><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="56cf9840-07e2-4539-8362-37f25e856605"><ac:rich-text-body><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Note that </span><strong style="text-align: left;">listen_address</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> and </span><strong style="text-align: left;">broadcast_rpc_address</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> are still set to their respective machine’s address</span>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Now that you understand what a seed is and how to configure it, follow the instructions to install and configure Cassandra on Windows or Linux operating system. </p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Installing Cassandra on Windows" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Windows instructions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Installing Apache Cassandra" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Linux instructions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p>After installing and configuring all nodes, start the seed node. Verify that it is operational by issuing the command &quot;nodetool status&quot;.</p><p>Once the seed node is operational, you can proceed to start the other nodes in the cluster. You must allow the node to join the cluster before proceeding to the next node. After all nodes have started, the &quot;nodetool status&quot; command will display results as shown below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba7bbd5e-a49a-46c3-8349-e4a3910a5243"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Datacenter: datacenter1
=======================
Status=Up/Down
|/ State=Normal/Leaving/Joining/Moving
--   Address     Load      Tokens    Owns (effective)    Host ID                                   Rack
UN   10.1.1.101  6.7 GB    256       100%               b33c603a-95c7-426d-9f7b-ebad2375086a      rack1
UN   10.1.1.102  6.34 GB   256       100%               16f40503-4a65-45fe-9ee7-5d942506aa87      rack1
UN   10.1.1.103  6.15 GB   256       100%               2d90c119-08a4-4799-ac73-0440215d0b18      rack1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07a6a9fe-7183-468b-aa2e-dd0217632e83"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Node Consistency</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>In order to ensure that all nodes are consistent, please run the &quot;nodetool repair&quot; command nightly. This can be scheduled as a crontab job or via Windows Task Scheduler. This only needs to be executed on a single node.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Cassandra configuration parameters<strong> </strong></h4><p><em>- </em><strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver.basic.contact-points</span></strong></p><p>The value of this parameter is a list of Cassandra node IP addresses. You can find this parameter in <strong>application.conf </strong>by looking for the following.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb80014f-e4c6-416b-891e-4851c286dc34"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver {
        basic {
                contact-points = ["10.1.1.101:9042", "10.1.1.102:9042", "10.1.1.103:9042"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><em>- </em><strong>esi.persistence.cassandra.keyspace.replication-factor</strong></p><p>This parameter defines the Cassandra replication factor which describes how many copies of your data will be written by Cassandra. For example, replication factor 2 means your data will be written to 2 nodes.</p><p>For a three-node cluster, if you would like the cluster to be able to survive 1 node loss, you will need to set the replication factor to 3. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ca7bf66d-9414-408c-ad38-4b3c0ef35458"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[persistence {
         cassandra {
                 keyspace {                                                                                           
                        replication-factor = 3                                         
                 }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Please note that this configuration will be used only for the first time <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>connects to Cassandra and creates a new twc keyspace. Changing the replication factor after the keyspace has already been created is rather a complex task. Read <a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/cql/3.3/cql/cql_using/useUpdateKeyspaceRF.html">this document</a> if you need to change it.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e13764cb-5f63-4445-b3ab-7bba06e12951"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>uses QUORUM for both write and read consistency levels.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra/3.0/cassandra/dml/dmlAboutDataConsistency.html">Click here</a> for a detailed explanation of data consistency.</p><p>To start up the Teamwork Cloud cluster, start the server on the seed machine and wait until you see a message similar to the following in the <strong>server.log</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1f276482-11df-41b8-adc2-991a35f44f62"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[INFO  2017-02-15 10:57:08.409 Teamwork Cloud cluster with 1 node(s) : [10.1.1.111] [com.nomagic.esi.server.core.actor.ClusterHealthActor, twcloud-esi.actor.other-dispatcher-31]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Then you can start the server on the remaining machines. You should see the following messages in the <strong>server.log</strong> which shows all 3 nodes are forming the cluster.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f5609385-9fcf-4ed9-b15b-b7abaee171f1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[INFO  2017-02-15 10:58:23.956 Teamwork Cloud cluster with 2 node(s) : [10.1.1.111, 10.1.1.112] [com.nomagic.esi.server.core.actor.ClusterHealthActor, twcloud-esi.actor.other-dispatcher-18]
INFO  2017-02-15 10:58:25.963 Teamwork Cloud cluster with 3 node(s) : [10.1.1.111, 10.1.1.112, 10.1.1.113] [com.nomagic.esi.server.core.actor .ClusterHealthActor, twcloud-esi.actor.other-dispatcher-18]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Setting up a product cluster</h3><p>Before setting up a Teamwork Cloud cluster, you need to determine the following.</p><ul><li>The initial number of nodes in the cluster.</li><li>The IP address of each Teamwork Cloud node.</li><li>Prepare a list of IPs of Cassandra nodes.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>The instructions below use the following sample environment for ease of understanding.</p><ul><li>3 nodes Teamwork Cloud cluster.</li><li>Teamwork Cloud node IP addresses are <em>10.1.1.111, 10.1.1.112</em>, and <em>10.1.1.113.</em></li><li>All nodes will be used as seed nodes.</li><li>IP addresses of Cassandra nodes are <em>10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102, 10.1.1.103.</em></li></ul><p><br /></p><p>Setting up the Teamwork Cloud cluster involves 2 groups of parameters in <strong>application.conf</strong>.</p><h4>Teamwork Cloud clustering parameter</h4><p><em>- </em><strong>akka.cluster.seed-nodes</strong></p><p>This parameter indicates the initial seed for the cluster.</p><p>If you install <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>using the installer file, you will be asked to provide the seed node IP during installation process and the value will be configured in <strong>application.conf</strong>.</p><p>If you install using the zip file, you will need to manually configure the parameter in<em> </em><strong>application.conf</strong>. Search for the following:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8352ec9-5ab7-4507-9aac-c749a7d3aa57"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seed-nodes = ["akka://twcloud@${seed-node.ip}:2552"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Replace <em>${seed-node.ip}</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">with the IP addresses of the seed nodes, so it should look similar to the following:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8b0f896d-e1ba-4460-b63c-990b3a372a79"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seed-nodes = ["akka://twcloud@10.1.1.111:2552","akka://twcloud@10.1.1.112:2552","akka://twcloud@10.1.1.113:2552"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046530 space=MCS version=2 -->
## PAGE 00610: Cassandra cluster operations

- page_id: `247046530`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046530/Cassandra+cluster+operations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-12T13:03:54.000+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Installation > Advanced installation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Replacing the down node

To replace a down/unresponsive node with a new node (different address) in the cluster, prepare the replacement node configuration to match other cluster nodes. Then, start the replacement node by specifying the dead node to replace with.

Prepare the replacement node by configuring it with the same cluster name, seed nodes, and data center name as other nodes in the cluster.

Add the following parameter to JVM_OPTS in *cassandra-env.sh* (default location: */etc/cassandra/conf*) for the replacement node:

```text
]]>
```

This parameter will drop the dead node and add the new node to the cluster upon startup. Remove the parameter after successful startup and replacement node joins the cluster.

To monitor the state and progress of replacement operations (including move and remove), use this command to display the progress of streaming operations:

```bash

```

##### Dropping node

Before dropping a node from a live cluster, make sure there are enough live nodes in the cluster to maintain replication factor. If dropping a node that is down, refer to [Replace Down Node] procedure.

To drop a live node, run the following command while on the node:

```bash

```

To drop a dead/offline node, run the following command:

```bash
]]>
```

If the dropped node was a seed node, update cassandra.yaml and reload seeds on all live nodes by executing the following command:

```bash

```

Otherwise, Teamwork Cloud service may not start.

##### Modifying the seed node

If a seed node was added, removed, or replaced from a cluster, the seed node list will need to be updated.

Use the following command to check what seeds are currently configured for the cluster:

```bash

```

Update the *seeds* list configuration, under *seed_provider* of *cassandra.yaml*, for every node in the cluster. Use the following command to apply the seed list change on a live node.

```bash

```

Otherwise, the changes will not be applied until each node is restarted.

##### Syncing nodes

The Cassandra repair tool is used to fix data inconsistencies between nodes. If a down node was replaced, repair will stream differences in the data from out-of-sync data between nodes.

For incremental repair of the current node, use the command:

```bash

```

Incremental repairs should be run regularly on each node. Apache recommends doing this every 7 days.

For a full repair of all data, use the command:

```bash

```

A new node to the cluster should bootstrap and sync data from other nodes. If a new node has not streamed data from other nodes on the cluster, use the following command to initiate the process:

```bash

```

For additional information, refer to the [official Cassandra documentation](https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/4.1/index.html).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Replacing the down node</h3><p><span>To replace a down/unresponsive node with a new node (different address) in the cluster, prepare the replacement node configuration to match other cluster nodes. Then, start the replacement node by specifying the dead node to replace with.</span></p><p><span>Prepare the replacement node by configuring it with the same cluster name, seed nodes, and data center name as other nodes in the cluster.</span></p><p><span>Add the following parameter to JVM_OPTS in <em>cassandra-env.sh</em> (default location: <em>/etc/cassandra/conf</em>) for the replacement node:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9db496b5-a4a1-48f7-87a1-3891eb11abcf"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-Dcassandra.replace_address_first_boot=<dead_node_ip>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>This parameter will drop the dead node and add the new node to the cluster upon startup. Remove the parameter after successful startup and replacement node joins the cluster.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To monitor the state and progress of replacement operations (including move and remove), use this command to display the progress of streaming operations:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="deaf9a63-18e4-473c-b01f-d23c438c0b33"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool netstats]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Dropping node</h3><p><span>Before dropping a node from a live cluster, make sure there are enough live nodes in the cluster to maintain replication factor. If dropping a node that is down, refer to [Replace Down Node] procedure.</span></p><p><span>To drop a live node, run the following command while on the node:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f51aec9-ea78-4d51-899a-e1b078f2a2f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool decommission]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>To drop a dead/offline node, run the following command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="24993ddf-c7ac-49b9-8878-59ee2165c1f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool removenode <Host ID>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>If the dropped node was a seed node, update cassandra.yaml and reload seeds on all live nodes by executing the following command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="faa70e07-b94c-4122-a6d9-7b20aac818b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool reloadseeds]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Otherwise, Teamwork Cloud service may not start.</span></p><h3>Modifying the seed node</h3><p><span>If a seed node was added, removed, or replaced from a cluster, the seed node list will need to be updated. </span></p><p><span>Use the following command to check what seeds are currently configured for the cluster:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2a0f40e3-7083-4696-a166-d4538ec1ab4d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool getseeds]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>Update the <em>seeds</em> list configuration, under <em>seed_provider</em> of <em>cassandra.yaml</em>, for every node in the cluster. Use the following command to apply the seed list change on a live node. </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ea76ee70-4443-4395-8f3f-22a6771277a6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool reloadseeds]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>Otherwise, the changes will not be applied until each node is restarted.</span></p><h3>Syncing nodes</h3><p><span>The Cassandra repair tool is used to fix data inconsistencies between nodes. If a down node was replaced, repair will stream differences in the data from out-of-sync data between nodes. </span></p><p><span>For incremental repair of the current node, use the command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f00da33-99f1-45f2-93c5-7e17da232a29"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool repair]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>Incremental repairs should be run regularly on each node. Apache recommends doing this every 7 days.</span></p><p><span>For a full repair of all data, use the command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4d2520ec-1407-4bb4-97cf-c122c67befc2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool repair --full]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span> </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">A new node to the cluster should bootstrap and sync data from other nodes. If a new node has not streamed data from other nodes on the cluster, use the following command to initiate the process:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7f54edcd-5f1e-4d59-b4c7-e6502f3af87c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool rebuild]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span>For additional information, refer to the <a href="https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/4.1/index.html">official Cassandra documentation</a>.</span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046517 space=MCS version=2 -->
## PAGE 00611: Cassandra Data Migration

- page_id: `247046517`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046517/Cassandra+Data+Migration
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-12T12:37:29.410+02:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page provides guidance and procedures for migrating falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwcdata stored in Apache Cassandra to a new server or cluster. The procedures outlined here provide a clean method for migrating data between databases without carrying over Cassandra configuration artifacts from the source server, such as the cluster name and number of tokens.

The migration procedures use two utility scripts provided with the falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwcinstallation. These scripts can be found in *<install_root>/Utilities/AdminTools/db-tools* of your installation.

***truncate_tables.sh***

Truncate all tables for keyspaces specified in the script. By default, this script will truncate all tables in the product *twc* keyspace. The script can be used to truncate the database remotely by setting *PRIVATE_IP*.

***stream_sstables.sh***

Stream the *twc* keyspace table data to a new Cassandra database using the sstableloader tool. Provide target node address and full path to source data (where *twc* folder is located) as commandline options. Source table data must be stored under "*twc*" directory and locally accessible by the script. Use *localhost* or 127.0.0.1 as the target address if streaming to a local node.

**Prerequisites**

- Local execution access to *cqlsh*is required for both scripts.
- Target node(s) with newly-installed Cassandra and empty database
- Network connection between source and target nodes is needed for remote data streaming and/or transfer.
- Elevated system permission may be required to stream data.
- Gawk Linux package

##### Cluster to cluster (same size)

Migrating data between clusters with the same number of nodes involves direct node-to-node streaming of the data. This procedure also applies to single-node data migration (one to one). Configure the target cluster with a different cluster name than the source cluster. Before attempting data migration, make sure all target cluster nodes are up and normal. Perform the steps below for each pair of source and target nodes.

**On source node**

1. Stop the twcloud service (if running on node).
2. Perform nodetool drain.
3. Stop the cassandra service.
4. Create a copy of the twc directory (default: /data/data/twc ) and move to target node.

**On target node**

1. Install Apache Cassandra.
2. Start the cassandra service.
3. Install falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwc .
4. Configure falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwc with Cassandra cluster information, including replication factor.
5. Start the twcloud service to create an initial twc keyspace and tables.
6. Stop the twcloud service.
7. Execute truncate_tables.sh to truncate all twc keyspace tables.
8. Execute stream_sstables.sh to stream the source data to the target node.

##### Cluster to single node

Before attempting data migration, make sure the target node has sufficient storage capacity. The target node will initially be loaded with data from all source nodes. Database size will reduce after duplicate data is cleaned up with scheduled compaction.For example, if the source cluster has 3 nodes and 5GB of data on each node, then the target node will initially have up to 15GB of data after data is streamed from each source node.

**On source nodes**

1. Stop the twcloud service (if running on node).
2. Perform nodetool drain.
3. Stop the cassandra service.
4. Create copy of the twc directory (default: /data/data/twc ) and move to target node.

**On target node**

1. Install Apache Cassandra.
2. Configure with different cluster name in cassandra.yaml.
3. Start the cassandra service.
4. Install falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwc .
5. Start the twcloud service to create an initial twc keyspace and tables.
6. Stop the twcloud service.
7. Execute truncate_tables.sh to truncate all twc keyspace tables.
8. Execute stream_sstables.sh to stream source data to target node.

After data has finished streaming, the target node will initially contain duplicate data from all source cluster nodes. The duplicate data will eventually be cleaned up with scheduled compaction. To force compaction immediately, run the following command:

```bash

```

##### Single node to cluster

To migrate data from one node to a cluster, data only needs to be streamed to one node in the cluster. All other nodes in the cluster will replicate the migrated data. Before attempting data migration, make sure all target cluster nodes are up and normal. Select a target node to perform data migration on.

**On source node**

1. Stop the twcloud service (if running on node).
2. Perform nodetool drain.
3. Stop the cassandra service.
4. Create a copy of the twc directory (default: /data/data/twc ) and move to target nodes.

**On selected target node**

1. Install Apache Cassandra.
2. Start the cassandra service.
3. Install falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwc .
4. Configure falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwc with Cassandra cluster information, including replication factor.
5. Start the twcloud service to create an initial twc keyspace and tables.
6. Stop the twcloud service.
7. Execute truncate_tables.sh to truncate all twc keyspace tables.
8. Execute stream_sstables.sh to stream source data to target node.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="ee69e8d3-3a07-4beb-8bf1-8cd8492882b7">This</ac:inline-comment-marker> page provides guidance and procedures for migrating <span><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="846e459b-4cd8-4b23-b59c-81cb5f8bfb38" ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Magic Collaboration Studio</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">mcs</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="9dfe864f-aa44-425c-b298-c602306a9b73" ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Teamwork Cloud</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">twc</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></span>data stored in Apache Cassandra to a new server or cluster. The procedures outlined here provide a clean method for migrating data between databases without carrying over Cassandra configuration artifacts from the source <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="883aa330-55e4-4492-888d-49a6c4b2cba0">server</ac:inline-comment-marker>, such as the cluster name and number of tokens. </span></p><p><span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9cb1d829-a27c-4e8e-9e46-261c77905670">The migration</ac:inline-comment-marker> procedures use two utility scripts provided with the <span><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="da4b428d-2489-47d9-8a5c-1d6dccd12099" ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Magic Collaboration Studio</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">mcs</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="135daa68-e8d0-4e61-8a4f-56d306f9c3a3" ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Teamwork Cloud</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">twc</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></span>installation. These scripts can be found in <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9cf27a9a-ec12-41c7-8935-188b63d872f6">Utilities/AdminTools/db-tools</ac:inline-comment-marker></em> of your installation.</span></p><p><strong><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9c0255ad-cd3b-4e4a-a443-31174a0f8949">truncate_tables.sh</ac:inline-comment-marker></em></strong></p><p>Truncate all tables for keyspaces specified in the script. By default, this script will truncate all tables in the product <em>twc</em> keyspace. The script can be used to truncate the database remotely by setting <em>PRIVATE_IP</em>.</p><p><strong><em>stream_sstables.sh</em></strong></p><p>Stream the <em>twc</em> keyspace table data to a new Cassandra database using the sstableloader tool. Provide target node address and full path to source data (where <em>twc</em> folder is located) as commandline options. Source table data must be stored under &quot;<em>twc</em>&quot; directory and locally accessible by the script. Use <em>localhost</em> or 127.0.0.1 as the target address if streaming to a local node.</p><p><br /></p><p><strong style="letter-spacing: -0.003em;">Prerequisites</strong></p><ul><li><span>Local execution access to <em>cqlsh </em>is required for both scripts.</span></li><li><span>Target node(s) with newly-installed Cassandra and empty database</span></li><li><span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4acbe6b8-681f-4763-b0f7-449941cf0d9e">Network connection between source and target nodes is needed for remote data streaming and/or transfer.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></li><li><span>Elevated system permission may be required to stream data.</span></li><li><span>Gawk Linux package</span></li></ul><h3>Cluster to cluster (same size)</h3><p><span>Migrating data between clusters with the same number of nodes involves direct node-to-node streaming of the data. This procedure also applies to single-node data migration (one to one). Configure the target cluster with a different cluster name than the source cluster.
