<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046529 space=MCS version=4 -->
## PAGE 00675: Deploying Cassandra Multi-Data Center

- page_id: `247046529`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046529/Deploying+Cassandra+Multi-Data+Center
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-06-25T10:37:59.047+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Installation > Advanced installation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud2022x Refresh2 and later versions support Apache Cassandra multiple data centers (Multi-DC) capability. Multi-DC Cassandra ensures continuous availability of data to Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudusers. There are two main scenarios for using multiple data centers: live backup/disaster recovery and geographical location. The deployment configuration of a live backup data center is presented here as an example.

Please note that Multi-DC deployment requires in-depth knowledge of Cassandra architecture and administration. Incorrect deployment or operation can result in total data loss. Technical support is not available, as this is considered a custom deployment strategy.

##### Scenarios for deploying a Multi-DC Cassandra cluster

###### Live backup/disaster recovery

Multiple data centers can be deployed at different physical locations as a disaster recovery solution. In this situation, one Cassandra data center would be the primary access point, while an off-site secondary data center would continuously receive the latest database updates. If the primary data center goes down, data can be restored from the secondary data center by bringing new Cassandra nodes online at the primary data center. Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudcan also be reconfigured to connect to the secondary data center as a temporary solution.

In this scenario, there is no degradation in performance. Read and write operations are performed on the primary data center first, then eventually synced to the second data center as a background operation.

###### ****Geographical location

For organizations with users spread across multiple geographical locations, it may be desirable to optimize data access by location. Each region can have a dedicated data center. All of the data centers would form one Cassandra cluster, and all of them would have access to the latest data.

In this scenario, performance would mainly be affected by the database write operations. Write operations would be applied to all data centers. Read operations would remain local and thus not affect performance.

##### Deploying Multi-DC Cassandra for the product

This is the recommended configuration procedure for deploying Multi-DC Cassandra for Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud.

To configure Multi-DC Cassandra cluster

1. 
2. Start Cassandra cluster and verify all data centers are online.
3. 
4. 
5. For an existing database, .
6. Start Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudservices and verify operation.

##### Configuring Cassandra cluster with multiple data centers

Our example deployment is a single Cassandra cluster with two data centers located in separate geographical locations. Each Cassandra data center has three nodes. When performing the initial installation and configuration of Cassandra nodes, make sure the version of Cassandra is consistent across all nodes. Also, make sure to synchronize clocks across all nodes.

Start by updating the *cassandra.yaml* configuration file.

Set a unique cluster name for all nodes. This deployment is a single cluster spanning multiple data centers. The cluster name should be the same across all nodes.

```text

```

Set the seed provider for all data centers. Assign at least two seed nodes for each data center. Specify the address for all seeds. Alternatively, use a seed provider service to keep track of seed node addresses.

```text

```

Then, change the endpoint snitch implementation on each node. The default snitch is *SimpleSnitch*, which does not support Multi-DC. Cassandra uses snitch to gather network topology information. In this case, we are using *GossipingPropertyFileSnitch* for Multi-DC setup. Cassandra will obtain data center information from the *cassandra-rackdc.properties* file when *GossipingPropertyFileSnitch* is set.

```text

```

Refer to Cassandra documentation if you are updating snitch for an exiting database: [https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra-oss/3.0/cassandra/operations/opsSwitchSnitch.html](https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra-oss/3.0/cassandra/operations/opsSwitchSnitch.html)

Edit the *cassandra-rackdc.properties* file for each node. All nodes in a data center should have the same “dc” name.

For the primary data center:

```text

```

For the backup data center:

```text

```

Start each Cassandra node. Port 7000 is the default inter-node communication (encrypted and unencrypted) port for a Cassandra cluster. Make sure port 7000 is open for communication between data centers. Please note that the initial connection between data centers can take some time. Run *nodetool status* to confirm that all data centers have connected to the cluster.

For an existing Cassandra cluster, run *nodetool repair* and *nodetool cleanup* after the above configurations have been made.

###### Configuring the product for Multi-DC

Once all Cassandra nodes are online and data center connections are confirmed, configure the *application.conf* file for Multi-DC.

In the *esi.persistence.cassandra.keyspace* section, we have added the following parameters for our example. With 3 nodes in each data center, the replication factor is set to 3. The consistency level is set to “FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL" for a disaster recovery scenario. Please refer to the table below for consistency setting options. If deploying an entirely new database, the “twc” keyspace will be created with the replication strategy and factors specified here. For an existing database, you will have to manually edit the keyspace replication parameters (see Cassandra Keyspace Configuration below).

```text

```

- When *is-multi-dc* is set to *true*, the *replication-factor* parameter is ignored.
- The *dc-replication-factors* configuration will only be applied when creating a new “twc” keyspace. If there is an existing “twc” keyspace in Cassandra, Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudwill use the existing replication factor set in the database. Use *ALTER KEYSPACE* to change the replication factor.

| Keyspace Consistency Setting | Write Consistency Level | Read Consistency Level |
| --- | --- | --- |
| BEST_READ_PERFORMANCE_GLOBAL | ALL | ONE |
| BEST_READ_PERFORMANCE_LOCAL | ALL | LOCAL_ONE |
| BEST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL | EACH_QUORUM | EACH_QUORUM |
| FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL | EACH_QUORUM | LOCAL_QUORUM |
| BEST_RELIABILITY_LOCAL | LOCAL_QUORUM | LOCAL_QUORUM |

In the *esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver.basic* section, set the data center name and contact points to local values. The local data center will be used as the primary database for client requests.

```text

```

Use the seed nodes from the local data center specified in *cassandra.yaml*

```text

```

###### Configuring Cassandra keyspace

If you are deploying Multi-DC with an existing Cassandra database, you will need to manually reconfigure the replication parameters for the “twc” keyspace.

Use *cqlsh*, the Cassandra command-line interface, to modify “twc” keyspace replication parameters.

```bash

```

Run *nodetool repair* after keyspace modification to update all nodes.

To verify replication in *cqlsh*:

```bash

```

These steps can also be used to create the “twc” keyspace. Note that manual keyspace creation is not needed, as Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudwill create the keyspace during initial startup.

```bash

```

##### Multi-DC Cassandra Best Practices

- The Cassandra version must be the same across all nodes and data centers
- At least two seed nodes should be assigned for each data center
- Clocks should be synchronized for all node

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>2022x Refresh2 and later versions support Apache Cassandra multiple data centers (Multi-DC) capability. Multi-DC Cassandra ensures continuous availability of data to <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>users. There are two main scenarios for using multiple data centers: live backup/disaster recovery and geographical location. The deployment configuration of a live backup data center is presented here as an example. </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Please note that Multi-DC deployment requires in-depth knowledge of Cassandra architecture and administration. Incorrect deployment or operation can result in total data loss. Technical support is not available, as this is considered a custom deployment strategy.</span></p><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Scenarios for deploying a Multi-DC Cassandra cluster</span></h3><h4><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Live backup/disaster recovery</span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Multiple data centers can be deployed at different physical locations as a disaster recovery solution. In this situation, one Cassandra data center would be the primary access point, while an off-site secondary data center would continuously receive the latest database updates. If the primary data center goes down, data can be restored from the secondary data center by bringing new Cassandra nodes online at the primary data center. <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>can also be reconfigured to connect to the secondary data center as a temporary solution.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In this scenario, there is no degradation in performance. Read and write operations are performed on the primary data center first, then eventually synced to the second data center as a background operation. </span></p><h4><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong> </strong>Geographical location</span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For organizations with users spread across multiple geographical locations, it may be desirable to optimize data access by location. Each region can have a dedicated data center. All of the data centers would form one Cassandra cluster, and all of them would have access to the latest data. </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In this scenario, performance would mainly be affected by the database write operations. Write operations would be applied to all data centers. Read operations would remain local and thus not affect performance.</span></p><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Deploying Multi-DC Cassandra for the product</span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">This is the recommended configuration procedure for deploying Multi-DC Cassandra for <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud</span>.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">To configure Multi-DC Cassandra cluster</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span><ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring Cassandra cluster with multiple data centers"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Configure Cassandra cluster with more than one data center.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li><li>Start Cassandra cluster and verify all data centers are online.</li><li><span><ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring the product for Multi-DC"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Configure the product for Multi-DC.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li><li><span><ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring authentication in Web Application Platform for Multi-DC"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Configure authentication in Web Application Platform for Multi-DC.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li><li><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For an existing database</span>, <ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring Cassandra keyspace"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[update the “twc” keyspace for Multi-DC setup]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Start <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud</span>services and verify operation.</span></li></ol><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Configuring Cassandra cluster with multiple data centers</span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Our example deployment is a single Cassandra cluster with two data centers located in separate geographical locations. Each Cassandra data center has three nodes. When performing the initial installation and configuration of Cassandra nodes, make sure the version of Cassandra is consistent across all nodes. Also, make sure to synchronize clocks across all nodes.  </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Start by updating the <em>cassandra.yaml</em> configuration file. </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Set a unique cluster name for all nodes. This deployment is a single cluster spanning multiple data centers. The cluster name should be the same across all nodes.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="74e767c7-e04a-4920-9421-eb5ba65a2644"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[cluster_name: 'MDC Cluster']]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Set the seed provider for all data centers. Assign at least two seed nodes for each data center. Specify the address for all seeds. Alternatively, use a seed provider service to keep track of seed node addresses.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9f21423e-fe82-4b53-b0a4-2354e7fe0f05"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seed_provider:
    - class_name: org.apache.cassandra.locator.SimpleSeedProvider
      parameters:
          - seeds: "10.0.0.1, 10.0.0.2, 10.0.1.1, 10.0.1.2"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Then, change the endpoint snitch implementation on each node. The default snitch is <em>SimpleSnitch</em>, which does not support Multi-DC. Cassandra uses snitch to gather network topology information. In this case, we are using <em>GossipingPropertyFileSnitch</em> for Multi-DC setup. Cassandra will obtain data center information from the <em>cassandra-rackdc.properties</em> file when <em>GossipingPropertyFileSnitch</em> is set. </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fc269aa0-2fed-430b-a612-84d77f41ce2c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[endpoint_snitch: GossipingPropertyFileSnitch]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Refer to Cassandra documentation if you are updating snitch for an exiting database: <a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra-oss/3.0/cassandra/operations/opsSwitchSnitch.html">https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra-oss/3.0/cassandra/operations/opsSwitchSnitch.html</a></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Edit the <em>cassandra-rackdc.properties</em> file for each node. All nodes in a data center should have the same “dc” name.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For the primary data center:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2f9ec96e-2868-4b44-99ed-f47bc4134781"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[dc=datacenter1
rack=rack1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For the backup data center:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ec0d8678-6728-4ff6-99b5-3e3aa70a6088"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[dc=datacenter2
rack=rack1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Start each Cassandra node. Port 7000 is the default inter-node communication (encrypted and unencrypted) port for a Cassandra cluster. Make sure port 7000 is open for communication between data centers. Please note that the initial connection between data centers can take some time. Run <em>nodetool status</em> to confirm that all data centers have connected to the cluster.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For an existing Cassandra cluster, run <em>nodetool repair</em> and <em>nodetool cleanup</em> after the above configurations have been made.</span></p><h4><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Configuring the product for Multi-DC</span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Once all Cassandra nodes are online and data center connections are confirmed, configure the <em>application.conf</em> file for Multi-DC. </span></p><p><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the <em>esi.persistence.cassandra.keyspace</em> section, we have added the following parameters for our example. With 3 nodes in each data center, the replication factor is set to 3. The consistency level is set to “FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL&quot; for a disaster recovery scenario. Please refer to the table below for consistency setting options. If deploying an entirely new database, the “twc” keyspace will be created with the replication strategy and factors specified here. For an existing database, you will have to manually edit the keyspace replication parameters (see Cassandra Keyspace Configuration below).</span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6c8b0f69-b6b7-4080-b030-dccd9259860e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[is-multi-dc = true
strategy-class = "NetworkTopologyStrategy"
consistency = "FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL"
dc-replication-factors {
     datacenter1=3
     datacenter2=3
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">When <em>is-multi-dc</em> is set to <em>true</em>, the <em>replication-factor</em> parameter is ignored. </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">The <em>dc-replication-factors</em> configuration will only be applied when creating a new “twc” keyspace. If there is an existing “twc” keyspace in Cassandra, <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>will use the existing replication factor set in the database. Use <em>ALTER KEYSPACE</em> to change the replication factor.</span></li></ul><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Keyspace Consistency Setting</span></p></th><th><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Write Consistency Level</span></p></th><th><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Read Consistency Level</span></p></th></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_READ_PERFORMANCE_GLOBAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">ALL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">ONE</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_READ_PERFORMANCE_LOCAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">ALL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_ONE</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">EACH_QUORUM</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">EACH_QUORUM</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">EACH_QUORUM</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_QUORUM</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_RELIABILITY_LOCAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_QUORUM</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_QUORUM</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the <em>esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver.basic</em> section, set the data center name and contact points to local values. The local data center will be used as the primary database for client requests.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e5434990-4ea4-4c49-91c3-f17b93684643"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[load-balancing-policy.local-datacenter = datacenter1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Use the seed nodes from the local data center specified in <em>cassandra.yaml</em></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="882ea537-923b-4bdf-86fd-cb7e8f683dee"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[contact-points = ["10.0.0.1:9042","10.0.0.2:9042"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Configuring Cassandra keyspace</span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">If you are deploying Multi-DC with an existing Cassandra database, you will need to manually reconfigure the replication parameters for the “twc” keyspace.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Use <em>cqlsh</em>, the Cassandra command-line interface, to modify “twc” keyspace replication parameters.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e609f3d-bc25-49be-afb6-538060dec874"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALTER KEYSPACE twc WITH replication = {'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy', 'datacenter1' : 3, 'datacenter2' : 3};]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Run <em>nodetool repair</em> after keyspace modification to update all nodes.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">To verify replication in <em>cqlsh</em>:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fd6ba8d6-7cf0-4045-a8cd-b57634c6bb58"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[select * from system_schema.keyspaces;]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">These steps can also be used to create the “twc” keyspace. Note that manual keyspace creation is not needed, as <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>will create the keyspace during initial startup.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7c7f50b5-4d1c-4b90-90f0-d972fed3e78a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[CREATE KEYSPACE twc WITH replication = {'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy', 'datacenter1' : 3, 'datacenter2' : 3}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Multi-DC Cassandra Best Practices</span></h3><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">The Cassandra version must be the same across all nodes and data centers</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">At least two seed nodes should be assigned for each data center</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Clocks should be synchronized for all node</span></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046666 space=MCS version=6 -->
## PAGE 00676: Deployment configuration

- page_id: `247046666`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046666/Deployment+configuration
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-12-22T09:23:16.267+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Configuration
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The configuration settings described in this section are optional. You only need to configure them if the default setting is not suitable for your environment. The Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudsystem will load the configuration file every time it starts. Therefore, changes to the configuration file will be applied once you restart it.

The Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudservice configuration file (*application.conf)* is located in *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/application.conf.*This file is in HOCON format ("Human-Optimized Config Object Notation"). For more information about HOCON file format, visit the following link [https://github.com/typesafehub/config/blob/master/HOCON.md#syntax](https://github.com/typesafehub/config/blob/master/HOCON.md#syntax).

To configure Cassandra authentication, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Hardening Cassandra' space=''].

##### Setting a password for authentication with the Authentication Server

Teamwork Cloud Admin uses an Authentication Server to authenticate users. Teamwork Cloud Admin needs a password to access /token endpoint of the Authentication Server. You need to specify the same password that is entered in the *webappplatform.properties* file ****(the **authentication.client.secret** parameter). The default value is **CHANGE_ME**.

```text

```

##### Changing a log file location

You can configure the logging system of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudby using *logback.xml*.

To change the name or location of the log files in *logback.xml*, locate <file> fields. When changing the log file name and location, you need to be sure that the user account for your Operating System (OS) that you will use to start Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudhas the write permission in the newly configured location. To learn more about logback configuration, see [http://logback.qos.ch/manual/configuration.html](http://logback.qos.ch/manual/configuration.html).

##### Changing the client port

The default port number of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudfor the client to connect to is **3579**. However, the port number is changeable. If you would like to change the default port number, for example, to 3580, you can configure this in the Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudconfiguration file, *application.conf.* Search for the content shown below, and edit as follows:

```text

```

Please note that the modeling tool, such as MagicDraw, is considered a client of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud. Consequently, if you change this setting, you have to specify the port number while connecting from the modeling tool.

##### Setting a public IP

If Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudis deployed in the environment where the client will connect to the server via a public IP (NATed to a private IP), set the parameter **server-broadcast-host** to the public IP address and **host** to the private IP. Search for the content in *application.conf* and edit as follows:

```text

```

##### Configuring secure SSL communication between Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudand Cassandra

You can set up secure communication between Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudand Cassandra as described below.

To set up SSL communication between Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudand Cassandra

1. Configure application.conf :
  1. Locate and open the application.conf file.
  2. Search for the following section: /Certs/keystore.p12"
 keystoreType = ""
 keystorePassword = ""
 truststorePath = "/Certs/keystore.p12"
 truststoreType = ""
 truststorePassword = ""
 }
}]]>
  3. Configure the properties, as shown in the example above: 
**enabled** = *true***keystorePath** = *<install_root>/Certs**/keystore.p12***keystoreType** = *<keystore_type>***keystorePassword** = *<keystore_password>***truststorePath** = *<install_root>/Certs**/keystore.p12***truststoreType** = *<truststore_type>***truststorePassword** = *<truststore_password>*
  4. Save the changes.
2. Configure *cassandra.yaml*:
  1. Locate and open the *cassandra.yaml*file.
  2. Configure the following properties: 
**client_encryption_options**: 
enabled: *true*optional:*false* If*enabled* and *optional* are set to true, encrypted and unencrypted connections are handled. **keystore**: *<path_to_keystore>/keystore.p12***keystore_password**: *<keystore_password>***require_client_auth**: *false*
  3. If r equire_client_auth is set to true , set truststore and truststore_password : truststore : <path_to_truststore>/keystore.p12 truststore_password : <truststore_password> The **require_client_auth** setting is optional – it adds an extra layer of security by requiring the client to present its own certificate to the server (mutual TLS). However, this setting does not affect whether encryption is used for communication: if **require_client_auth**is set to *false*, the server does not require the client to present a certificate, but the communication is still encrypted as long as SSL/TLS is enabled.
  4. Use these advanced defaults: protocol : TLS algorithm : SunX509 store_type : PKCS12

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5636081d-0dc9-4394-bb64-6f48a6008088">The configuration settings</ac:inline-comment-marker> described in this section are optional. You only need to configure them if the default setting is not suitable for your environment. The <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>system will load the configuration file every time it starts. Therefore, changes to the configuration file will be applied once you restart it.</p><p>The <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>service configuration file (<em>application.conf)</em> is located in <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration/application.conf. </em>This file is in HOCON format (&quot;Human-Optimized Config Object Notation&quot;). For more information about HOCON file format, visit the following link <a style="text-decoration: underline;" href="https://github.com/typesafehub/config/blob/master/HOCON.md#syntax">https://github.com/typesafehub/config/blob/master/HOCON.md#syntax</a>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f9b2b143-f82f-4f1d-a043-74b5ef04ca4e"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To configure Cassandra authentication, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Hardening Cassandra" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Setting a password for authentication with the Authentication Server</h3><p>Teamwork Cloud Admin uses an Authentication Server to authenticate users. Teamwork Cloud Admin needs a password to access /token endpoint of the Authentication Server. You need to specify the same password that is entered in the <em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">webappplatform.properties</span></em> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e6ad671d-3f49-44ee-8255-860643be0576">file</ac:inline-comment-marker> <strong> </strong>(the <strong>authentication.client.secret</strong> parameter). The default value is <strong>CHANGE_ME</strong>. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2ec852e3-c3af-4242-866d-b502f6a8761b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">authserver.properties</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.console {
....
#
 # The console client password parameter.
 client {
 pswd = "CHANGE_ME"
 }
...
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Changing a log file location</h3><p class="Standard">You can configure the logging system of <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>by using <em>logback.xml</em>.</p><p class="Standard">To change the name or location of the log files in <em>logback.xml</em>, locate &lt;file&gt; fields. When changing the log file name and location, you need to be sure that the user account for your Operating System (OS) that you will use to start <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>has the write permission in the newly configured location. To learn more about logback configuration, see <a href="http://logback.qos.ch/manual/configuration.html">http://logback.qos.ch/manual/configuration.html</a>.</p><h3><span>Changing the client port</span></h3><p class="Standard">The default port number of <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>for the client to connect to is <strong>3579</strong>. However, the port number is changeable. If you would like to change the default port number, for example, to 3580, you can configure this in the  <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>configuration file, <em>application.conf. </em> Search for the content shown below, and edit as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60d6afce-0f48-4698-92e2-f938c6735f91"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[net
	{
		connectors = [
			{
				# The ip address to bind to (for client connection).
				# The default value is bind to all IPs.
				host = "0.0.0.0"
				
				# Port for client application to connect to. The default value is 3579.
				# This is corresponding to the port specified when connecting from client such as MagicDraw. 
				port = 3580]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="Standard">Please note that the modeling tool, such as MagicDraw, is considered a client of <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud</span>. Consequently, if you change this setting, you have to specify the port number while connecting from the modeling tool.</p><h3>Setting a public IP</h3><p>If <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>is deployed in the environment where the client will connect to the server via a public IP (NATed to a private IP), set the parameter <strong>server-broadcast-host</strong> to the public IP address and <strong>host</strong> to the private IP. Search for the content in <em>application.conf</em> and edit as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="651c7dc5-daf9-4c7d-aad4-53e077a72453"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[net
	{
		connectors = [
			{
				# The ip address to bind to (for client connection).
				# The default value is bind to all IPs.
				host = "0.0.0.0"
				
				# Port for client application to connect to. The default value is 3579.
				# This is corresponding to the port specified when connecting from client such as MagicDraw. 
				port = 3579
				
				protocol = "raw"
			}
		]
		
		# To support deploying Teamwork Cloud in private network, but client connects from public network,
		# Specify IP address that will be used by client to connect to this Teamwork Cloud from public network.
		# This value will be default to esi.net.host if it is empty.
		# server-broadcast-host is not recommended to use.
		server-broadcast-host = "PUBLIC_IP_ADDRESS"
	}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Configuring secure SSL communication between <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>and Cassandra</h3><p>You can set up secure communication between <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>and Cassandra as described below.</p><p>To set up SSL communication between <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>and Cassandra</p><hr /><ol><li>Configure <em>application.conf</em>:<ol><li>Locate and open the <em>application.conf</em> file.</li><li><p>Search for the following section:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c0d712b-9728-492f-bf52-d223edbdda83"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# Secure connection with SSL between Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud
esi.security {
    cassandra {
        enabled = true
    	keystorePath = "<install_root>/Certs/keystore.p12"
    	keystoreType = "<keystore_type>"
    	keystorePassword = "<keystore_password>"
    	truststorePath = "<install_root>/Certs/keystore.p12"
        truststoreType = "<truststore_type>"
        truststorePassword = "<truststore_password>"
    }
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Configure the properties, as shown in the example above:<br /><strong>enabled</strong> = <em>true<br /></em><strong>keystorePath</strong> = <em style="text-align: left;">&lt;install_root&gt;/Certs</em><em>/keystore.p12<br /></em><strong>keystoreType</strong> = <em>&lt;keystore_type&gt;<br /></em><strong>keystorePassword</strong> = <em>&lt;keystore_password&gt;<br /></em><strong>truststorePath</strong> = <em style="text-align: left;">&lt;install_root&gt;/Certs</em><em>/keystore.p12<br /></em><strong>truststoreType</strong> = <em>&lt;truststore_type&gt;<br /></em><strong>truststorePassword</strong> = <em>&lt;truststore_password&gt;</em></p></li><li>Save the changes.</li></ol></li><li><p>Configure <em>cassandra.yaml</em>:</p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Locate and open the <em>cassandra.yaml </em>file.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Configure the following properties:<br /><strong>client_encryption_options</strong>:<br />enabled: <em>true<br /></em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">optional: </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">false</em></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3ae01f62-5e93-4391-b9cb-ad67953781e2"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If<em> enabled</em> and <em>optional</em> are set to true, encrypted and unencrypted connections are handled.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>keystore</strong>: <em>&lt;path_to_keystore&gt;/keystore.p12<br /></em><strong>keystore_password</strong>: <em>&lt;keystore_password&gt;<br /></em><strong>require_client_auth</strong>: <em>false</em></p></li><li>If <strong>r</strong><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="05045098-50bf-4bf1-9332-ec56391ac38a">equire_client_auth</ac:inline-comment-marker> </strong>is set to <em>true</em>, set <em>truststore</em> and <strong>truststore_password</strong>:<br /><strong>truststore</strong>: <em>&lt;path_to_truststore&gt;/keystore.p12<br /></em><strong>truststore_password</strong>: <em>&lt;truststore_password&gt;<br /></em><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60ada203-675f-4cbe-8e5c-2f67bb634424"><ac:rich-text-body>The <strong>require_client_auth</strong> setting is optional – it adds an extra layer of security by requiring the client to present its own certificate to the server (mutual TLS). However, this setting does not affect whether encryption is used for communication: if <strong>require_client_auth </strong>is set to <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">false</span></em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, the server does not require the client to present a certificate, but the communication is still encrypted as long as SSL/TLS is enabled.</span></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Use these advanced defaults:<br /><strong>protocol</strong>: <em>TLS<br /></em><strong>algorithm</strong>: <em>SunX509<br /></em><strong>store_type</strong>: <em>PKCS12</em></li></ol></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046576 space=MCS version=50 -->
## PAGE 00677: Deployment example on Kubernetes

- page_id: `247046576`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046576/Deployment+example+on+Kubernetes
- version_number: 50
- version_date: `2026-06-29T13:20:18.150+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Enterprise deployment
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This deployment example is not intended for production use. Use it for testing purposes only.

The versions of all falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloud and servicestwccomponents used in this deployment are listed on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page. Before you begin, verify that you are using compatible versions of all required components. Using versions not listed on that page is not tested and may result in unexpected behavior. 
 
This chapter provides information on how to deploy the product for testing purposes. Keep in mind that the guidelines provided below are not considered the best practices and do not take into account security settings and password encryption. This deployment has been tested with a specific Kubernetes version, CNI, container runtime, and OS. If you use other solutions, use them at your own risk.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- Kubernetes cluster (for more information, refer to https://kubernetes.io/ ):
  - 1 virtual machine used as the control plane node and 3 nodes used as worker nodes The names of worker nodes must match their FQDN.
  - Control plane node: 4 CPU cores, 8GB RAM
  - Worker node: 16 CPU cores, at least 64GB RAM (depends on your deployment size )
- Kubernetes version: v1.35.4 or later
- Container runtime: Containerd (for more information, refer to https://containerd.io/ )
- CNI: Calico
- Load Balancer: MetalLB (the configuration instructions are provided later in this chapter) Most cloud providers do not support MetalLB.
- IP: 1 additional IP address with the DNS A record that points to this IP (IP from your network that is not used by your cluster nodes).
- Ingress: HAProxy Kubernetes Ingress Controller (with sticky session support).
- A Host with Docker or Podman to build images and push them to the image registry.
- An image registry (repository) such as Harbor, Sonatype Nexus, Gitlab, or Docker registry server to push and pull the images you will build.
- The applications needed to run Teamwork Cloud and services (the installation steps are described below.):
  - Zookeeper (for more information, refer to https://zookeeper.apache.org/ )
  - Apache Artemis MQ (for more information, refer to [https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/](https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/))
  - Keda CRD (deployed with the Simulation web application)
  - MetalLB
  - HAProxy Ingress
  - Cassandra

**See the compatible versions of the applications in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page.**

The following figure displays the environment used in this deployment example.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this environment, the K8S cluster admin host (Linux virtual machine) is used to build images, run the image repository, and manage the Kubernetes cluster.

**In the workflow below:**

- admin.example.com refers to the K8S cluster admin host and should be changed to your K8S cluster admin FQDN.
- ingress.example.com refers to the domain name through which Web Application Platform will be accessible.
- All the steps are performed on the K8S cluster admin host.
- All commands are prepared to work in the user's home folder. If needed, adapt the commands to meet your needs.

To deploy the product for testing purposes

1. Install kubectl as described in https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/install-kubectl-linux/ .
2. Install the helm to the K8S cluster admin host as described in https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install .
3. Use the following command (or another way) to copy cluster config files to the K8S cluster admin host: bashRDark:.kube ~
]]> Then open to edit *~/.kube/config* and replace the localhost IP address with your control plane IP address or hostname, e.g., *https://127.0.0.1:6443*.
4. Prepare the required files and directories:
  1. Download and extract the [ATTACHMENT filename='twc-services-charts-2026xRefresh1.zip'] file.
  2. Download the installation package for your product version. For more information, refer to Downloading installation files .
  3. Extract the packaged files, and inside the ~/*twc-services-charts* directory (where you extracted the twc-services-charts*.zip* file) do the following:
    - Extract the *twcloud_<version>_no_install_linux64.zip*file. After extracting the files, you should have the following folder structure: ~/twc-services-charts*/CATIANoMagicServices/TeamworkCloud/*.
    - Extract the*Web_Application_Platform_<version>_war_files.zip*file. You will find all files mentioned in this chapter in *twcloud_<version>_no_install_linux64.zip* and *Web_Application_Platform_<version>_war_files.zip,*or on the software download website (except for Teamwork Cloud keystore and SSL certificate):The ~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/conf directory contains server.xml.The ~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf directory contains other configuration files.The ~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/data directory contains files for Cameo Collaborator and document-exporter.The ~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/TeamworkCloud/configuration directory contains Teamwork Cloud configuration files.The ~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/Utilities/Cassandra contains the .jar file for Cassandra's communication with Zookeeper.The ~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps directory contains application .war files.
5. Go to the ~/twc-services-charts directory and execute the following command to generate a private key and self-signed certificate. Change the command by entering your DN identifiers. bashRDark
6. Execute the following command to create a PKCS#12 keystore. If you change the name or password, do not forget to change them in the configuration files. For more information about the CA certificate and P12 or PFX files, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing SSL certificate' space=''] . bashRDark The *tls.crt* and *tls.key* files will be used for HAProxy Ingress Controller, so file names in HAProxy Ingress Controller must be the same.
7. Execute the following commands to prepare the *helm-charts root* directory: bashRDark
8. Copy the generated certificate files to the appropriate locations by executing the following commands: bashRDark
9. [Download the Apache Cassandra files](https://cassandra.apache.org/_/download.html) to the twc-services-charts directory. You can find the compatible Cassandra version for this release on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page.
10. Execute the following commands to extract the Cassandra *.tar* file: bashRDark-bin.tar.gz
cd ~/twc-services-charts/apache-cassandra-/conf]]>
11. In the *cassandra.yaml* file edit Cassandra settings as shown below.
12. Execute the following command to copy files to the *twc-services-charts/helm-charts/configs/cassandra* directory: bashRDark
13. Execute the following command to copy the Authserver file to the appropriate location: bashRDark
14. Execute the following commands to copy the Tomcat's file to the appropriate location: bashRDark
15. Edit the application.conf file as shown below.
16. Execute the following commands to edit the jvm.options file: bashRDark
17. Execute the following commands to copy Web Application Platform files to the appropriate location: bashRDark
18. Ensure all files listed below exist in their directories: auth*authserver.properties*cassandra*cassandra.yaml**logback.xml*ssl*tls.crt**tls.key*tomcat*catalina.properties**server.xml*twcloud*application.conf**logback.xml**jvm.options*twcloud/ssl*keystore.p12*web*revision.txt**log4j2.properties**log4j2simulation.properties**webappplatform.properties*
19. Execute the following command to create a directory for each application you are going touse: bashRDark
20. Copy all files listed below to their directories (except the artemis directory): admin-console*admin.war**Dockerfile**ROOT* (The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)authentication*authentication.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)collaborator*collaborator.war**Dockerfile**data*(The directory is located in~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices*/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/*. Remove the*document-exporter*directory from the*data*directory after copying.)*ROOT* (The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)document-exporter*document-exporter.war**Dockerfile**data* (This directory is located in *<webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/.*Remove the *collaborator* directory from the *data* directory after copying.)*ROOT* (The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)magiclab*magiclab.war**Dockerfile**ROOT* (The directory is located in *~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)magiclabcollab*magiclab-collaborator.war**Dockerfile**ROOT* (The directory is located in *~/twc-services-charts/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)oslc*oslc.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)reports*reports.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)resource-usage-map*resource-usage-map.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)resources*resources.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)sgi-crawler*sgi-crawler.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)simulation*simulation.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)sysmlv2-api*sysmlv2-api.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)webapp*webapp.war**Dockerfile**ROOT*(The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps*.)twcloud*Dockerfile**TeamworkCloud* (The directory is located in ~/twc-services-charts/*CATIANoMagicServices*.)
21. Execute the following command to start the local Docker registry: bashRDark In this case, a local Docker registry is run on the K8S cluster admin host. It uses port 5000, so make sure this port is open on your host firewall and accessible to your Kubernetes cluster. This local Docker registry should be used for testing purposes only and is not considered production-ready. In the production environment, run the image registry with TLS and authentication.
22. Add your repo URL (FQDN of the K8S cluster admin host) to the Docker /etc/docker/daemon.json file.
23. If the *daemon.json*file does not exist, create it. Assuming there are no other settings in the file, it should have the following content:
24. Execute the following command to restart the Docker service: bashRDark
25. Execute the commands below for each service that you want to build images for inside their own directories, as shown in the examples in substeps a, b, and c. bashRDark {IMAGE_REPO_URL} - your image repository URL. It can differ depending on the image registry provider.{APP_NAME} - application name.{VERSION} - version to be used for the tag.
  1. The following command example builds images for Admin Console: bashRDark
  2. The following command example tags the built image: bashRDark
  3. The following command example pushes the image to the registry: bashRDark Check the names of the local repositories after Docker images are pushed. 
curl -s [http://admin.example.com:5000/v2/_catalog](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/_catalog) 
curl -s [http://](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/webapp/tags/list)[admin.example.com](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/_catalog)[:5000/v2/<repository_name>/tags/list](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/webapp/tags/list) 
Example: 
curl -s [http://](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/webapp/tags/list)[admin.example.com](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/_catalog)[:5000/v2/webapp/tags/list](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/webapp/tags/list) 
REGISTRY_URL="[http://](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2)[admin.example.com](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/_catalog)[:5000](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2/_catalog)[:5000/v2](http://kns-rre6twctst.dsone.3ds.com:5000/v2)"; curl -s $REGISTRY_URL/_catalog | jq -r '.repositories[]' | xargs -I {} sh -c 'echo "Repository: {}"; curl -s '"$REGISTRY_URL"'/{}/tags/list | jq -r ".tags[]"'
26. To be able to pull images to your Kubernetes cluster nodes, add your image repository to the Containerd configuration on all cluster nodes :
  1. Edit the*/etc/containerd/config.toml*file by adding the following lines to the "mirrors" part: Keep in mind that alignment is very important, as shown in the example below. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  2. Execute the following command to restart Containerd services on all cluster nodes: bashRDark
27. Execute the following commands to add dependency repos: bashRDark
28. Execute the following command to update chart dependencies: bashRDark
29. Execute the following command to install Custom Resource Definitions (CRD). This will create all services anddeployments. bashRDark You can check the status of the services and pods with this command: kubectl get all -n keda Keda and MetalLB should be added as separate resources.Since CRDs (being a globally shared resource) are fragile, you have to assume that once a CRD is installed, it is shared across multiple namespaces and groups of users. For that reason, installing, modifying, and deleting CRDs is a process that has ramifications for all users and systems of that cluster.
30. Execute the following command to deploy MetalLB (skip this step if MetalLB is already deployed and configured): bashRDark
31. To check the MetalLB deployment status, execute the following command on the control plane node or the K8S cluster admin host: bashRDark You should see an output similar to this: 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] If you see that the MetalLB status is 'Running,' but READY is 0/1, give it some time to start and repeat the command.
32. Create configuration files for MetalLB as instructed below. Make sure to complete all the substeps of this step to create and apply both configurations. Otherwise, the product will not work properly.
  1. Your IT department should give you a reserved IP address or a range of IP addresses (depending on your needs) with a DNS A record that points to this IP address.At least one IP address with a domain name should be reserved for Ingress.Configuration files should be formatted in "*yaml".*The first IP address configured in *metallb_ipaddresspool.yaml* will be assigned for Ingress.Edit the *~/twc-services-charts/helm-charts/crd/**metalLB/metallb_ipaddresspool.yaml* file as shown below:
  2. Edit (if necessary) the *~/twc-services-charts/helm-charts/crd/**metalLB/l2advertisement.yaml* file as shown below. You can change the metadata name as needed. Keep in mind that the ipAddressPool name should match the specifications.
  3. Execute the following command to apply the MetalLB configuration: bashRDark
  4. Execute the following command to check the applied configuration: bashRDark
  5. Execute the following command to create the MetalLB advertisement: bashRDark
  6. Execute the following command to check the advertisement configuration: bashRDark
33. Install HAProxy CRD
  - bashRDark
34. Find the values.yaml file in the parent helm chart ( ~/twc-services-charts/helm-charts/values.yaml ) and provide the values of the parameters in the file to enable or disable specific applications or parts of the configuration. All parameters marked with the MANDATORY comment in the values.yaml file must be configured before deployment . Parameters without this marker are optional and may be modified as needed to customize the deployment. When configuring parameters, follow the guidelines below: Image repositories — Set the repository name for each application image to match exactly the name registered in your image registry. To verify the available repository names, query your registry catalog: curl -s [http://admin.example.com:5000/v2/_catalog](http://admin.example.com:5000/v2/_catalog) 
Use the names returned by this command as the repository values in *values.yaml*. Names are case-sensitive and must match exactly. Image tags — Tag names are set to latest by default. If you used a different tag when pushing images to the registry (see Step 25), set the tag to match. To verify which tags are available for a given repository, run: curl -s http://admin.example.com:5000/v2/<repository_name>/tags/list shellExample: Ingress host — If ingress is enabled in values.yaml , the host field must be set to the fully qualified domain name (FQDN) that resolves to your Ingress external IP address (the DNS A record configured earlier, e.g., ingress.example.com ). Leaving this field empty or incorrectly set will prevent routing to the web applications.
35. Do one of the following:
  - To deploy services to the default namespace, execute this command in the helm parent chart directory (*you can change "twc" to any release name*): bashRDark
  - To create a namespace and deploy services in this namespace, execute this command in the helm parent chart directory : bashRDark This helm chart includes Zookeeper and HAProxy ingress as dependencies. They will be deployed automatically.
36. After all web applications are deployed, execute the following command to check their status: bashRDark All pods should be READY:1/1, STATUS:Running.The HAProxy Ingress Controller service should be Type:LoadBalancer.EXTERNAL-IP should match the address that you provided in the MetalLB configuration.If pods do not run, check for problems by executing this command:kubectl describe pod pod_name
37. Execute the following command to check Ingress rules: bashRDark You should get an output similar to this: TLS:
 tls-secret terminates insgress.example.com
Rules:
 Host Path Backends
 ---- ---- --------
 nm-wap10639.dsone.3ds.com
 /admin webapp-adminconsole:8443 (10.233.88.83:8443)
 /authentication webapp-authentication:8443 (10.233.88.84:8443)
 /collaborator webapp-collaborator:8443 (10.233.73.213:8443)
 /document-exporter webapp-docexporter:8443 (10.233.88.86:8443)
 /reports webapp-reports:8443 (10.233.88.88:8443)
 /resources webapp-resources:8443 (10.233.73.217:8443)
 /magiclab webapp-magiclab:8443 (10.233.73.215:8443)
 /magiclab-collaborator webapp-magiclabcollab:8443 (10.233.105.15:8443,10.233.73.216:8443,10.233.88.89:8443)
 /webapp webapp-webapp:8443 (10.233.73.218:8443)
Annotations: haproxy.org/cookie-persistence: rtns-cookie
 haproxy.org/server-ssl: true
 haproxy.org/timeout-tunnel: 3600s
 meta.helm.sh/release-name: webapp
 meta.helm.sh/release-namespace: wap
Events: ]]>
38. Test the product deployment:
  1. In an internet browser, go to *[https://ingress.example.com/webapp](http://ingress.example.com)* (the DNS A record that points to the Ingress external IP reserver earlier). If you added a DNS record to bind the domain name to the IP address, you can use the domain name instead of the IP. The browser should show a warning because of the self-signed certificate. Accept it to proceed, and you will be redirected to the Teamwork Cloud Authentication web page. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  2. Log in with your credentials, and you will be redirected back to Web Application Platform.
